lesson.title: Restaurant Service & Management
lesson.explanation: Front-of-house service creates the customer experience. Table service: greet within 30 seconds, present menu, take orders (starters first), serve from left, clear from right. Menu planning: cost food (aim 30-35% food cost), balance variety and simplicity. Stock control: FIFO (first in, first out), weekly stocktake, par levels. POS systems process orders, track sales, manage bills. Staff scheduling: cover peaks (12-2pm lunch, 6-9pm dinner). In SA: tipping culture (10-15%), liquor licence required for alcohol service.

examples.problem: Practical scenario in Restaurant Service & Management
examples.answer: Follow Restaurant Service & Management best practices

practice_problems.question: A restaurant serves 80 covers on a Friday evening. The menu has 12 mains. By 20:00, you've sold 30 portions of the most popular dish but the kitchen only prepped 35. Service continues until 22:00. How do you manage this situation without disappointing customers?
practice_problems.answer: Immediate actions: (1) Communicate with the kitchen: confirm exactly how many portions remain (5). At current sales rate (~10/hour), the dish sells out by 20:30. (2) Brief the waiting staff: 'We have 5 portions of [dish] remaining. When a guest orders it and we're out, suggest [alternative 1] and [alternative 2] with enthusiasm, not apology.' Train them to say: 'That's been incredibly popular tonight -- it's just sold out. May I recommend the [alternative], which our chef is particularly proud of?' (3) Consider 86ing the dish on the POS system after the last 5 portions to prevent taking orders that can't be fulfilled. (4) Upsell alternatives proactively: if a table is still deciding, the waiter can mention the alternatives before the guest asks for the sold-out dish. Prevention for next time: (1) Review sales data: if this dish consistently sells 30+ on Fridays, prep 40-45. (2) Set a par level with a buffer (20% above average Friday sales). (3) Consider pre-booking the dish for large parties. (4) Track 'lost sales' (how many times was the dish requested after 86) to justify higher prep quantities to the kitchen.
hints: The guest's experience of being told 'we're sold out' depends entirely on HOW it's communicated.
86'd items should trigger a management review: running out means lost revenue and disappointed guests.
practice_problems.explanation: Managing sold-out items is a routine service challenge. The goal is to turn a potential negative ('they don't have what I want') into a positive ('the waiter recommended something even better').
distractors: Just tell guests the dish is unavailable and let them choose
Keep taking orders and make the kitchen figure it out
Remove the dish from the menu permanently

practice_problems.question: Calculate the break-even point for a new restaurant. Monthly fixed costs: rent R35,000, salaries R85,000, utilities R8,000, insurance R3,000, loan repayment R12,000. Average spend per cover is R180, with a 65% gross margin (after food cost). How many covers per month must you serve to break even?
practice_problems.answer: Total fixed costs: R35,000 + R85,000 + R8,000 + R3,000 + R12,000 = R143,000/month. Contribution per cover: average spend R180 x 65% gross margin = R117.00 (this covers fixed costs after food cost is deducted). Break-even covers: R143,000 / R117 = 1,222 covers per month. Per day (assuming 26 operating days): 1,222 / 26 = 47 covers per day. This means: for a 60-seat restaurant, you need an average occupancy of 47/60 = 78% turnover per day, or you need 47 covers spread across lunch and dinner service. If lunch averages 15 covers and dinner averages 32 covers = 47 total = break-even. Everything above 47 covers per day contributes to PROFIT. At 60 covers/day: (60 - 47) x R117 = R1,521/day x 26 days = R39,546/month profit. Key insight: the break-even analysis shows that a restaurant must be consistently busy to survive. This is why location, marketing, and customer retention are as important as food quality.
hints: Break-even = fixed costs / contribution per unit. Contribution = revenue minus variable costs (food cost).
Most SA restaurants fail in the first 2 years. Understanding break-even BEFORE opening helps set realistic expectations.
practice_problems.explanation: Break-even analysis is essential for restaurant owners and managers. It quantifies exactly how busy the restaurant needs to be to survive, informing decisions about pricing, staffing, and marketing.
distractors: Break-even means the restaurant is profitable
Just divide total costs by selling price

practice_problems.question: A table of 6 has ordered. One guest has a severe nut allergy. They order the steak with a sauce that contains cashews. Describe the correct allergen management procedure.
practice_problems.answer: Procedure: (1) The waiter MUST inform the guest: 'The [sauce name] contains cashew nuts. Given your allergy, I strongly recommend an alternative sauce. May I suggest the peppercorn or mushroom sauce, which are nut-free?' Never assume the guest knows the ingredients. (2) If the guest chooses an alternative: communicate the allergy clearly to the kitchen via the POS system (ALLERGY flag) and verbally to the expeditor/chef. Write 'NUT ALLERGY' prominently on the order ticket. (3) Kitchen protocol: the chef must verify all ingredients in the chosen sauce (check labels for hidden nuts, e.g., 'may contain traces of tree nuts'). Prepare the dish using clean equipment -- not utensils or surfaces that have contacted nut-containing sauces. (4) Service protocol: the waiter confirms the correct dish with the chef before serving. Present the dish to the guest and verbally confirm: 'This is your steak with peppercorn sauce -- no nuts.' (5) If there is ANY doubt about cross-contamination, inform the guest and offer an alternative. (6) Document allergies in the booking system for repeat guests. Legal context: under the Consumer Protection Act (SA), restaurants have a duty of care. A severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis) can be fatal. Failure to manage allergens can result in criminal charges, civil liability, and closure.
hints: Nut allergies can be fatal. This is a life-or-death responsibility, not just a preference.
Cross-contamination in the kitchen is the biggest risk -- a shared spoon or cutting board can transfer enough allergen to trigger anaphylaxis.
practice_problems.explanation: Allergen management is a legal and ethical obligation. SA's Consumer Protection Act requires disclosure of allergens. Staff training on allergen protocols is mandatory for all food service establishments.
distractors: It's the guest's responsibility to know what's in the food
Just remove the nuts from the sauce
Cross-contamination only matters for severe allergies

practice_problems.question: A guest complains that their steak is overcooked (ordered medium-rare, received well-done). They are visibly angry. The restaurant is full. Handle this complaint using the LAST method (Listen, Apologise, Solve, Thank).
practice_problems.answer: LISTEN: Approach the table calmly. Make eye contact. Let the guest explain without interrupting. 'I completely understand. You ordered medium-rare and this is clearly well-done.' Acknowledge their frustration: 'I can see this is not what you expected, and I'm sorry.' Do not make excuses. APOLOGISE: 'I sincerely apologise. This is not our standard, and you deserve better.' Take ownership -- even if it was the kitchen's error, the guest is your responsibility. Never blame the chef in front of the guest. SOLVE: 'I'd like to send this back and have the chef prepare a new steak to medium-rare for you. I'll personally ensure it's correct. While you wait, may I offer you a complimentary starter or a glass of wine?' The replacement should be expedited (kitchen priority). When it arrives, personally check the temperature before serving. THANK: 'Thank you for your patience and for letting us make this right. I hope you enjoy the rest of your evening.' After the meal, check back: 'Was everything to your satisfaction? Thank you again for your understanding.' Manager consideration: offer a discount on the meal (10-20%) or comp the replacement steak. A R200 discount costs far less than losing a customer who tells 10 people about a bad experience.
hints: An angry guest who is handled well often becomes a MORE loyal customer than one who never had a problem. This is called the 'service recovery paradox.'
Never argue, blame, or make excuses. The guest doesn't care WHY the steak is wrong -- they care that you fix it.
practice_problems.explanation: Complaint handling is a defining skill in hospitality. The LAST method provides a structured approach that de-escalates the situation and turns a negative into a positive.
distractors: Argue that the steak looks medium-rare to you
Blame the kitchen in front of the guest
Offer a discount without replacing the steak

practice_problems.question: Create a weekly staff roster for a 60-seat restaurant open Tuesday-Sunday (closed Monday). Lunch: 12-3pm (3 waiters needed). Dinner: 6-10pm (5 waiters needed). Saturday has both services. You have 8 waiters, each working maximum 5 shifts per week. Ensure full coverage.
practice_problems.answer: Weekly shift requirements: Tue-Fri: dinner only = 5 waiters x 4 days = 20 shifts. Saturday: lunch (3) + dinner (5) = 8 shifts. Sunday: lunch only = 3 shifts. Total shifts needed: 20 + 8 + 3 = 31 shifts/week. Available: 8 waiters x 5 shifts max = 40 shifts available. Coverage is achievable with 9 shifts of slack. Sample roster: Waiter A: Tue-D, Wed-D, Thu-D, Sat-L, Sat-D (5 shifts). Waiter B: Tue-D, Wed-D, Fri-D, Sat-D, Sun-L (5 shifts). Waiter C: Thu-D, Fri-D, Sat-L, Sat-D, Sun-L (5 shifts). Waiter D: Tue-D, Wed-D, Thu-D, Fri-D, Sun-L (5 shifts). Waiter E: Tue-D, Wed-D, Fri-D, Sat-D (4 shifts). Waiter F: Thu-D, Fri-D, Sat-L, Sat-D (4 shifts). Waiter G: Tue-D, Thu-D, Sat-D (3 shifts -- part-time). Waiter H: Wed-D, Fri-D, Sat-D (3 shifts -- part-time). Key considerations: (1) Saturday needs both services -- schedule your strongest staff. (2) Rotate weekend shifts fairly. (3) Keep 1-2 waiters on call for busy nights or illness cover. (4) Basic Conditions of Employment Act: max 45 hours/week, minimum rest periods.
hints: Map out total shifts needed first, then allocate. Don't start with individual schedules.
Saturday double-shift (lunch + dinner with a break) counts as 2 shifts towards the weekly maximum.
practice_problems.explanation: Staff scheduling is one of the most time-consuming management tasks. Effective rostering balances coverage requirements, staff preferences, labour law compliance, and cost control.
distractors: Schedule all 8 waiters for every shift
Part-time staff are unreliable -- only use full-timers
The roster doesn't need to consider labour law

key_vocabulary.definition: Key concept in Restaurant Service & Management
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Applied in Restaurant Service & Management work

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for Restaurant Service & Management
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
